chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Bitso Strengthens Its Global Executive Team to Accelerate Expansion and Technological Leadership in Latin America - FF News
articles.snippet: Bitso Strengthens Its Global Executive Team to Accelerate Expansion and Technological Leadership in Latin America  FF News

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

articles.title: It's June. Are You the Leader Your January Goals Demanded? - Entrepreneur
articles.snippet: It's June. Are You the Leader Your January Goals Demanded?  Entrepreneur

post1.text:
Most leaders think scaling from $50M to $500M is a strategy problem. It is not. It is a leadership problem disguised as a strategy problem.

A recent Entrepreneur piece asked whether leaders in June are actually living up to the ambitions they set in January. For most, the answer is no. Not because the goals were wrong. Because the leader did not grow into the role the goals required.

Here is what that means for companies in the $50M to $500M range:

1. Your business will outgrow your leadership before it outgrows your systems. The org chart changes. The leader has to change first.

2. Accountability is not a quarterly review. It is a daily standard you set for yourself before you can set it for anyone else.

3. The executives who scale successfully stop managing outcomes and start managing their own behavior under pressure.

Leadership at this level is not about inspiration. It is about discipline, self-awareness, and being willing to be held to the same standard you hold your team.

So the real question: has your leadership kept pace with your growth targets this year?

post2.text:
A recent piece in HousingWire caught our attention. Buffini and Company just added three senior executives specifically to scale their coaching business.

That is not a coincidence. That is a deliberate leadership architecture decision.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern repeatedly with companies pushing past $50M. The leadership team that got you here is rarely the complete team that gets you to $500M. Scaling is not just about adding headcount. It is about adding the right executive capacity at the right growth stage before the gaps become crises.

If you are waiting until you feel the pain to upgrade your leadership team, you are already behind.

Read the full article and then ask yourself honestly: does your current executive bench match where your company is going?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMicEFVX3lxTE80SmJLNXdFS1VDcE9QNXQ2YzNFUDNUSzItcWljNWEyb2NEUVJ0UzdweEIzaVZyclAyWk5fcW54Nlo2U21GSVVoMHFJaVpLSFdaMU1tMXRIWU8wMTJvc3RJNTM5VkhoaVFxZnZFM0VlWVU

post3.text:
When Buffini and Company made headlines for adding senior executives to scale their coaching business, most people saw a hiring story. We saw a leadership story.

Chapter 1 of Rock Your Business, titled Lead Like Lives Depend On It and written by Katherine Slain, starts from a premise that makes most executives uncomfortable: the version of you that built a $50M company may not be the version capable of leading a $500M one.

Katherine does not ease you into that idea. She builds a framework around what it actually takes to lead at scale, and it starts well before you post that executive job opening.

The gap between where most growing companies stall and where they could go is almost always a leadership gap. Chapter 1 shows you how to close it.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The executives who scale companies past $500M are not necessarily the smartest people in the room. They are the ones who recognized, early enough, that their company had outgrown a previous version of their leadership and did something about it. Here is what that actually looks like in practice, and why most leaders miss the signal until it costs them.
posts.email_subject: Your company is scaling. Are you?

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: Business Exit Strategies - J.P. Morgan Private Bank
articles.snippet: Business Exit Strategies  J.P. Morgan Private Bank

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: The $14 Trillion Wealth Transfer: How To Buy Businesses From Retiring Owners | Adam Coffey Fabio Di Giannantonio (784seAhXWR) - Fathom Journal
articles.snippet: The $14 Trillion Wealth Transfer: How To Buy Businesses From Retiring Owners | Adam Coffey Fabio Di Giannantonio (784seAhXWR)  Fathom Journal

post1.text:
Most business owners preparing for an exit are optimizing the wrong thing.

They focus on revenue growth in the final years before a sale. Buyers focus on something else entirely: the quality and predictability of that revenue.

J.P. Morgan's Private Bank recently outlined how exit outcomes are shaped far earlier than most owners realize. The multiple you receive is not a reward for last year's numbers. It is a reflection of years of structural decisions.

Here is what that means practically for a $50M+ company:

1. Recurring revenue is valued at a premium over transactional revenue, sometimes dramatically so. Your revenue model is part of your valuation strategy.

2. Customer concentration kills multiples. If your top three clients represent more than 40% of revenue, sophisticated buyers will discount accordingly.

3. Management dependency on the owner is a liability that shows up in the deal structure, usually in the form of earnouts that shift risk back to you.

Your exit is not an event. It is the outcome of decisions you are making right now.

What would your business look like to a buyer today, if they opened the books tomorrow?

post2.text:
A recent piece from the U.S. Chamber of Commerce walks through how to build an exit strategy for your business. It is a solid primer, and it covers the core options: selling to a strategic buyer, private equity, management buyout, or passing to family.

But here is what that kind of article cannot tell you: at the $50M to $500M level, the exit strategy conversation is not about choosing a path. It is about building a company that earns access to the best version of each path.

Most owners start thinking about exits 12 to 18 months out. Buyers know this. And they price for it.

The companies that maximize their multiple start the work three to five years early, and they treat valuation as an operating discipline, not a transaction event.

If you are scaling and want to understand what actually moves the needle on your multiple, start here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMihAFBVV95cUxONERpMjFwUjkzVW8xLTRScUhVWTQ5VUFMS1R5cFY4c2p2eXYzZ3NYc0V0NUlaMmREUXM2N3VSaThYUjhERGNiSmhFTDB5TVBYNEhrOU9WSHRyYThWWUlUY2RYNlVFNkhvVGU0V3h1bXAtVzUwVVh0bG5FOWxmTUNJTTBJYjI

post3.text:
There is a $14 trillion wealth transfer underway as Baby Boomer business owners retire and sell. That number is staggering. And for middle-market companies in the $50M to $500M range, the window of opportunity on both sides of that transaction is wide open right now.

Chapter 2 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit. It was written by Paul Belair and George Hohman, two practitioners who have lived these deals from the inside.

One thing they make clear: most owners leave significant money on the table not because they made bad decisions at the closing table, but because they made avoidable structural mistakes years before they ever talked to a buyer.

The chapter gives you a framework for closing that gap before it costs you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: The moment most business owners start thinking about their exit is already too late to maximize their multiple. Valuation is not a number you negotiate at closing. It is a number you build over years through deliberate structural decisions. Here is what scaling companies between $50M and $500M need to understand about M&A before they ever take a buyer call.
posts.email_subject: Your exit multiple is set before you sell

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It - Forbes
articles.snippet: Why Leadership Teams Fail At Strategy Execution And How To Fix It  Forbes

articles.title: Strategy Update and 2025 Preliminary Results - WPP.com
articles.snippet: Strategy Update and 2025 Preliminary Results  WPP.com

articles.title: NAR’s strategic plan gets a tune-up as leadership pushes for faster results - HousingWire
articles.snippet: NAR’s strategic plan gets a tune-up as leadership pushes for faster results  HousingWire

post1.text:
Most strategic plans fail not because the strategy is wrong, but because leadership teams treat execution as someone else's job.

Forbes recently highlighted a pattern we see constantly with scaling companies: the gap between strategy creation and strategy ownership is where momentum dies. Leaders build a plan, hand it off, and wonder why nothing moves.

Here is what actually works for companies pushing past $50M:

1. Strategy needs a named owner at every level, not just a department. Accountability without a specific person attached to it is decoration.

2. Quarterly reviews are not enough. Fast-moving companies do rolling 30-day check-ins tied directly to annual goals. Drift gets caught early instead of at year-end.

3. The plan should create tension on purpose. If every leader is comfortable with their targets, the targets are too soft.

The companies that scale well do not have better strategies. They have better execution discipline built into their operating rhythm from the start.

What is the single biggest reason your last strategic plan stalled?

post2.text:
A recent piece in HousingWire covered how NAR is revising its strategic plan mid-cycle because leadership decided results were not coming fast enough.

That move deserves more attention than it is getting.

For companies scaling from $50M to $500M, the ability to adjust a strategic plan without abandoning it is a genuine competitive skill. Most leadership teams either hold the plan too rigidly or throw it out entirely when results lag. Neither works.

The companies we work with that scale fastest treat their strategic plan as a living document with fixed anchors and flexible tactics. They know the difference between a strategy that needs time and one that needs surgery.

If your team cannot answer that question clearly, that is the real problem.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidEFVX3lxTE5pazZGMi10akRud2hhYkFBMGhqa1J6eGI0d0VWQ09QNnJIQWtkQnVkNEgyYTE4UHJYaGppR1p0SW1NNmJTc25BUUFyM191SUpvRTFxLUJCTlIzNlBFUU9ZX3hmMkVKMW0ySkMyWm1qQnpiUTIy

post3.text:
Strategic planning looks very different when you are selling fish food versus Nike shoes. That is not a hypothetical. That is the foundation of Chapter 3 in our upcoming book, Rock Your Business.

Chapter 3, "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," was written by Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall. It draws on their direct experience running businesses in industries most consultants have never set foot in.

The insight that sticks: the planning process that works for a commodity business will quietly kill a brand-driven one, and most leadership teams never realize they are using the wrong model until growth stalls.

The chapter gives scaling companies a way to diagnose which type of business they are actually running before they build the plan, not after.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.
post3.text_note: No em dashes used. Book name and chapter title and authors named. Insight teased without full framework.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams spend weeks building a strategic plan and almost no time deciding who actually owns execution. That gap is where scaling companies stall. The research is consistent, the pattern is visible, and the fix is simpler than most executives want to admit. Here is what separates companies that execute from companies that just plan.
posts.email_subject: Your strategy isn't the problem. This is.

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: How to Double B2B Sales Growth - Bain & Company
articles.snippet: How to Double B2B Sales Growth  Bain & Company

post1.text:
Most companies at $50M don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. A habit is what your team does when no one is watching. A strategy is what drives predictable, scalable revenue growth. Bain & Company recently published research showing that companies who double B2B sales growth aren't doing more. They're doing fewer things with far more precision.

Here's what that means for leaders running $50M to $500M businesses:

1. Vague territory assignments and loosely defined ideal customer profiles are not strategy. They are hope with a quota attached.

2. Most sales teams optimize for activity, not outcomes. If your CRM tracks calls but not conversion logic, you are measuring the wrong thing.

3. A real sales strategy includes explicit decisions about who you will NOT sell to. Restraint at scale is a competitive advantage.

Every quarter you operate without a deliberate, documented sales strategy, you are effectively donating margin to your competitors.

What's one sales assumption your team has never actually pressure-tested?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Gartner caught our attention: AI time savings in sales won't translate to revenue growth unless chief sales officers actively intervene to redirect that saved time toward high-value selling activity.

That is a critical distinction for companies scaling past $50M.

Freed-up time is not the same as deployed capacity. If your sales team uses AI to move faster but still chases the wrong customers, you haven't gained leverage. You've just accelerated drift.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. The teams that win at scale pair smart tools with a clear strategy for where attention should actually go. Technology without targeting is just expensive noise.

If your organization is adopting AI in sales, ask yourself first: do we have the strategic clarity to direct what we're about to speed up?

Read the Gartner piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg?oc=5

post3.text:
A recent report from abasto.com made a pointed argument: B2B sales strategy now requires AI and personalization just to survive. We agree with the urgency. We'd push it further.

In Chapter 4 of Rock Your Business, titled "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation," authors Deb and Brandon lay out exactly why scaling companies bleed revenue not from bad salespeople, but from the absence of intentional strategy.

One insight they surface: most $50M-plus companies confuse sales process with sales strategy. Having a CRM and a pipeline review cadence is not the same as knowing precisely why you win, why you lose, and where you should be playing in the first place.

The cost of that confusion is not abstract. It shows up in your numbers every quarter.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your sales team isn't underperforming. Your sales strategy is missing. There's a difference that costs mid-market companies millions every year. New research from Bain and Gartner points to the same uncomfortable truth: activity without strategic clarity is just organized guessing. Here's what $50M-plus leaders need to confront before the next planning cycle.
posts.email_subject: Your sales team needs a strategy, not a pep talk

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: From BTG to The 1% Club: Tracking Nikhil Kamath’s investments across creators, communities and culture - Storyboard18
articles.snippet: From BTG to The 1% Club: Tracking Nikhil Kamath’s investments across creators, communities and culture  Storyboard18

articles.title: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez - PRWeek US
articles.snippet: Pride in PR: Gabriel Gomez  PRWeek US

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

post1.text:
Your marketing budget is not your biggest problem. Your brand identity is.

Most companies scaling past $50M keep running campaigns. More ads, more channels, more spend. But the businesses pulling away from the pack are doing something different: they are building around culture instead of campaigns.

JBL is a recent example. Rather than chasing seasonal pushes, they structured their marketing around the cultural moments and communities their audience already cared about. The result is a brand that compounds over time instead of resetting with every quarter.

For $50M+ leaders, three things worth considering:

1. Campaigns rent attention. Culture owns it. One scales cheaply, the other does not.

2. Your brand has a smell whether you designed it or not. Customers sense consistency or the lack of it before they can articulate why.

3. If your marketing disappears the moment you stop paying for it, you have distribution, not a brand.

So here is the real question: if your company went dark for 90 days, would anyone notice something was missing?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators caught our attention: JBL is deliberately shifting its marketing model away from traditional campaigns and toward sustained cultural relevance.

This is not a small tactical shift. It is a strategic rethinking of where brand equity actually lives.

For companies in the $50M to $500M growth range, this matters more than most leaders realize. At that stage, you are no longer just selling a product. You are building a reputation that either opens doors or closes them before your sales team ever makes contact.

The companies we work with that scale fastest tend to have one thing in common: their brand does work even when no one is actively running a campaign.

Read the full piece and ask yourself where your marketing dollars are actually building something lasting.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
JBL building its marketing around culture instead of campaigns is exactly the kind of strategic clarity that separates scaling companies from stalling ones.

Chapter 5 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled "What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?" and it is written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West for a reason. The chapter starts with a provocative premise: your brand already has a scent. The question is whether you chose it deliberately or let it develop by accident.

Kayli and Yasmin lay out what it actually takes to build a brand identity that holds up under the pressure of rapid growth, not just what looks good in a deck or wins an award.

One thing they make clear early: most marketing problems at this stage are not marketing problems at all.

Rock Your Business, by Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most growing companies treat branding as decoration and wonder why their marketing stops working the moment they pause the spend. The businesses scaling from $50M to $500M that actually pull away from the pack have figured out something simpler and harder: your brand is not what you say it is. It is what your market smells when you walk in the room.
posts.email_subject: Does your brand work when you stop paying?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: SRS Distribution Appoints Taylor Bradley as Director of Talent Acquisition - hrtoday.in
articles.snippet: SRS Distribution Appoints Taylor Bradley as Director of Talent Acquisition  hrtoday.in

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: Embark Names Russell Weaver as Chief People Officer to Lead Talent Strategy and Expansion - citybiz
articles.snippet: Embark Names Russell Weaver as Chief People Officer to Lead Talent Strategy and Expansion  citybiz

post1.text:
The best talent strategy for a scaling company is not about hiring more people. It is about hiring fewer, better-fit people with ruthless precision.

SHRM recently reported that hiring in 2026 is shifting away from volume-based recruiting toward highly targeted acquisition. Companies that chased headcount growth are now quietly rebuilding after costly misfires.

Here is what that means if you are running a $50M to $500M business:

1. Your hiring criteria need to reflect where the company is going, not where it has been. Job descriptions written for last year's org chart are filling seats with the wrong people.

2. Talent acquisition is not an HR function. At this stage of growth, it is a CEO and executive-level responsibility. The leaders who delegate this entirely are the ones who call us after the damage is done.

3. Speed-to-hire is not a success metric. Quality-of-retention at 18 months is.

Scaling companies that treat talent as a strategic asset rather than a resource problem consistently outperform their peers.

What is your current signal that a hire was the right one?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM lays out a clear shift in how competitive companies are approaching hiring in 2026: precision over scale, targeted over broad, quality over quantity.

This matters deeply for companies in the $50M to $500M growth corridor.

At that stage, one bad senior hire does not just cost a salary. It costs momentum, team cohesion, and often 12 to 18 months of organizational recovery time. The companies we work with that scale well treat every open seat as a high-stakes decision, not a pipeline exercise.

Building a Talent Engine that supports aggressive growth requires knowing exactly who you need before you start looking, not after.

Read the full SHRM piece and think about how your current hiring process stacks up: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
When Embark named a new Chief People Officer to lead talent strategy and expansion, it signaled something every growth-stage company eventually faces: at some point, how you find, develop, and retain people becomes the constraint on everything else.

Chapter 6 of our upcoming book Rock Your Business, called The Talent Engine, tackles exactly this. Written by Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West, the chapter goes beyond hiring frameworks and gets into how scaling companies build talent systems that can actually carry the weight of rapid growth.

One thing the authors make clear: the talent infrastructure that got you to $50M will not get you to $500M. The companies that figure this out early have a significant structural advantage over those that figure it out late.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling companies treat hiring as a response to growth. The ones that reach $500M treat it as the engine of growth. There is a structural difference between companies that chase headcount and companies that build talent systems, and that difference shows up clearly in the data. Here is what a real Talent Engine looks like at the $50M to $500M stage.
posts.email_subject: Why your hiring process caps your growth

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
More KPIs will not fix a broken business. They will bury it.

Most leadership teams at the $50M to $500M stage believe their performance problems stem from not measuring enough. So they add metrics. Then more metrics. Then dashboards nobody reads and reports nobody acts on.

Recent analysis from CLA CliftonLarsonAllen reinforces what we see with clients constantly: manufacturers who improve performance do not do it by tracking everything. They do it by identifying the handful of numbers that actually drive decisions.

Here is what high-growth companies do differently:

1. They tie every KPI directly to a strategic priority. No priority, no metric.
2. They kill metrics on a schedule. If a number has not changed a decision in 90 days, it gets cut.
3. They assign one owner per KPI. Shared ownership means no ownership.

The companies that scale are not the ones with the best dashboards. They are the ones with the clearest line between data and action.

How many of the metrics on your current scorecard would you defend if someone asked why each one exists?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Oracle NetSuite breaks down 30 financial metrics and KPIs worth tracking as companies head into 2026. It is a solid reference list, and for finance teams building out reporting infrastructure, it is genuinely useful.

But here is where scaling companies get into trouble: they treat a list like that as a checklist instead of a menu.

When you are growing from $50M toward $500M, the danger is not missing a metric. The danger is drowning your leadership team in numbers that feel important but do not connect to the decisions being made this quarter.

The discipline is subtraction, not addition. Pick fewer metrics. Own them completely. Then move.

If your team is building or rethinking your KPI framework, start here:
https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikwFBVV95cUxNcThuZllxNkVka0dpOV9QdHZQM2RiU0tfMGVIR05tdW9ZN1AtYngwQ1d4WDFSb3ZNSUxKNHFCSUR4aC1lR1g2ZWYzWFE0NWF1WmVpejAxOW9VRFdJa01yLUFKa1hvbjNGQnR6a2hZcVVtQzgwRXNCdXVGbjhfNWR2eWpTSlFyRk5zLVZ3MnJvMU14ZjA

post3.text:
There is a chapter in our upcoming book that every leadership team at the $50M to $500M stage needs to read before they build their next scorecard.

Chapter 7 of Rock Your Business is called Death by KPIs. It was written by Hector Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, and it tackles something most consulting advice ignores: the point at which your metrics stop driving performance and start suffocating it.

One of the core ideas the authors explore is that KPI overload is not a data problem. It is a leadership problem. The fix does not come from better software or a new dashboard template. It comes from the hard choices executives have to make about what actually matters.

That distinction changes how you run your business.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most growing companies do not fail because they lack data. They fail because they track everything and act on nothing. At the $50M to $500M stage, KPI overload is one of the most common and least talked about growth killers. Here is what separates the leadership teams that use metrics to scale from the ones that get buried by them.
posts.email_subject: Your KPIs might be the problem

articles.title: Top AI Time-Saving and Productivity Hacks From 12 CEOs and Bosses - Bloomberg.com
articles.snippet: Top AI Time-Saving and Productivity Hacks From 12 CEOs and Bosses  Bloomberg.com

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

articles.title: AI productivity gains are real but so is bad management: 'Leaders are really struggling to articulate what the vision and strategy is' - Fortune
articles.snippet: AI productivity gains are real but so is bad management: 'Leaders are really struggling to articulate what the vision and strategy is'  Fortune

post1.text:
AI will not save your business. Your strategy will.

Bloomberg recently surveyed a dozen CEOs about how they use AI to save time and boost output. The results were impressive. But here is the part no one is talking about: productivity gains mean nothing if your leadership team cannot articulate where the company is going.

Fortune put it plainly. Executives are struggling to define a clear vision and strategy, and AI is exposing that gap faster than it is closing it.

If you are running a $50M+ company, here is what this means for you:

1. AI amplifies execution. It does not replace direction. A confused team moving faster is still a confused team.

2. Your highest-leverage use of AI is buying back your thinking time, not your doing time. Use that time to sharpen strategy, not check more boxes.

3. The companies pulling ahead are not using more AI tools. They are using AI with greater clarity of purpose.

So before you roll out your next AI initiative, ask yourself: do we actually know where we are going?

#Leadership #ScalingBusiness #AIStrategy

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times explored a growing trend among busy executives: deploying AI versions of themselves to handle meetings, messages, and routine decisions.

Fascinating concept. But for companies scaling from $50M toward $500M, there is a more urgent question underneath it: which parts of your role actually require you, and which ones are just habit?

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who are often the biggest bottleneck in their own growth. AI twins do not solve that. Clear systems, smart delegation, and honest self-assessment do. AI just gives you the tool to get there faster.

If this topic is on your radar, the full article is worth your ten minutes. Read it here and then ask yourself where you are the constraint.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ

post3.text:
Fortune recently reported that AI productivity gains are real, but leaders are failing to connect them to a coherent vision. That tension sits at the heart of Chapter 8 in our upcoming book.

Chapter 8 is called The Third Monitor Theory, written by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain. It takes a hard look at how executives at scaling companies should actually be integrating AI into their day, not as a novelty, but as a core operating advantage.

One thing the chapter makes clear: the bottleneck is almost never the technology. It is the leader who has not decided what they are optimizing for.

If you are running a $50M+ business and trying to figure out how AI fits into your growth plan, this chapter will give you a framework worth keeping.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: AI is making your team faster. The question is whether they are moving in the right direction. New research shows that leaders at scaling companies are struggling to define vision and strategy clearly enough for AI to actually help. Here is what that means if you are trying to grow from $50M to $500M, and what to do about it before the gap gets wider.
posts.email_subject: AI is fast. Is your strategy ready?

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

articles.title: Impostor syndrome: the cost of being ‘superwoman’ at work and beyond - The Conversation
articles.snippet: Impostor syndrome: the cost of being ‘superwoman’ at work and beyond  The Conversation

post1.text:
The leaders most likely to derail your company's growth are not the incompetent ones. They are the ones who secretly believe they do not belong in the room.

Imposter syndrome does not disappear when you hit $50M in revenue. It gets louder. The stakes are higher, the scrutiny is sharper, and the gap between who you are and who you think you need to be feels wider than ever.

Here is what that actually costs you at scale:

First, leaders suffering in silence make risk-averse decisions. They protect their position instead of pushing the company forward.

Second, they under-delegate. They hold onto tasks they have already mastered because doing familiar work feels safer than owning unfamiliar authority.

Third, research from Seattle University reinforces that this pattern is especially acute in new or elevated leadership roles, where the pressure to prove legitimacy overrides the instinct to lead well.

The companies that scale past $500M are not led by people who conquered self-doubt. They are led by people who learned to perform at a high level despite it.

So what is your organization actually doing to surface this problem before it becomes a performance problem?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company makes a provocative argument: we should stop calling it imposter syndrome altogether. The label, they suggest, puts the problem on the individual when the environment often deserves more of the blame.

At Autobahn, we think that reframe matters enormously for companies scaling past $50M.

When your organization grows fast, leaders are routinely placed in roles that outpace their previous experience. If your culture treats the resulting self-doubt as a personal weakness rather than a structural signal, you will keep losing good people or watch them quietly underperform.

The question is not whether your leaders feel uncertain. They will. The question is whether your company is built to support them through that transition or punish them for it.

Read the full piece and think about what it means for your leadership pipeline.

post3.text:
Most leaders at the $50M mark have already beaten significant odds to get there. So why do so many of them feel like they are one bad quarter away from being found out?

Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business, titled How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It, takes that question seriously. Co-authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, the chapter goes beyond naming the problem and gets into the specific ways imposter syndrome reshapes decision-making, team dynamics, and long-term growth potential at the leadership level.

One thing the authors make clear early: the feeling of not deserving your seat at the table is not a sign that you do not belong there. But left unexamined, it will quietly influence every major call you make.

There is a way through it. The chapter shows you where to start.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: You built a company past $50 million. You have the results, the team, and the track record. So why does a part of you still feel like someone is about to figure out you do not know what you are doing? Imposter syndrome does not care about your revenue. At scale, it gets more sophisticated and more dangerous. Here is what it is actually costing your business.

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: I Quit My $390K Job to Start My Company. I Still Question My Decision. - Business Insider
articles.snippet: I Quit My $390K Job to Start My Company. I Still Question My Decision.  Business Insider

post1.text:
The best thing you can do for your business at $50M+ is decide what you actually want your life to look like.

Most founders never do this. They optimize for revenue, headcount, and market share, then wake up at $200M wondering why they feel empty. Morgan Stanley recently highlighted that entrepreneurs who sell their businesses often struggle far more with identity and purpose than with finances. The money lands. The meaning doesn't.

Here's what we see working with scaling companies:

1. Leaders who define personal success criteria before hitting growth milestones make better strategic decisions, not just better personal ones. Values clarity is a business asset.

2. Work-life design is not about balance. It's about intentional trade-offs. Know what you're trading and why.

3. The $50M to $500M window is when these decisions get hardest to reverse. Structure your life now, not after the next milestone.

You built something significant. But who are you building it for, and what does winning actually look like to you?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, covering Morgan Stanley's research on life after a business exit, makes something very clear: most entrepreneurs are financially prepared for what comes next and personally unprepared for all of it.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders scaling from $50M to $500M and beyond. The irony we see constantly is that the harder you drive toward the exit or the next growth milestone, the less you've thought about what you actually want on the other side.

Life planning is not a retirement conversation. It's a right-now conversation. The decisions you make about your time, your energy, and your identity during the scaling years shape everything that follows.

If you're building toward something big, start designing the life around it today, not after closing.

Read the full Fortune piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
A Business Insider story about a founder who left a $390K salary to build her own company, and still questions the decision, captures something our new book addresses directly.

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled 'Coming Up for Air,' was written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain. It tackles the part of scaling that no revenue chart captures: what kind of life are you actually designing while you build?

One thing the chapter makes clear is that coming up for air is not a reward you earn after hitting your number. It's a practice you build into the journey. Leaders who treat life planning as a business discipline perform differently, not just personally but organizationally.

The question the founder in that article is wrestling with is the right question. Most people just wait too long to ask it.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leaders at $50M and beyond have a growth plan, a hiring plan, and an exit plan. Almost none of them have a life plan. That gap is not a personal failure. It's a structural one. The scaling years are exactly when the trade-offs compound, and the window to make intentional choices narrows fast. Here's what the research and the experience of working with hundreds of scaling companies actually shows.
posts.email_subject: The plan most founders skip entirely
